results.translation: Likewise, counted within it [England] are principal towns with their fairs and markets: six hundred and forty-one; castles: one hundred and eighty-six; rivers: five hundred and fifty-four; and nine hundred and fifty-six bridges. The kingdom of Scotland has two archbishoprics and thirteen bishoprics; it is a land more rugged, cold, and less populous than that of England. Around this island there are many other smaller ones; one hundred and forty-five of them are inhabited, and the rest are bare rocks and crags.

results.original_text:
CAPITULO IV
De la sal

Así como este reino del Perú es el más abundante de metales, mayormente de plata, de cuantos sabemos hoy en el mundo, así también lo es de salinas naturales; que parece proveyó Dios de ellas con tanta abundancia donde tan necesaria es la sal para beneficiar los metales de plata, en que se consume una cantidad increíble de sal; y no hay provincia ni ciudad que padezca falta della; porque de la mucha que se saca en unas tierras, se reparte a otras y ninguna
results.translation:
CHAPTER IV
On Salt

Just as this kingdom of Peru is the most abundant in metals — above all in silver — of any known to us in the world today, so too is it in natural salt deposits [salinas]; for it seems God provided them in such abundance in a place where salt is so necessary for the processing [beneficio] of silver ores, in which an incredible quantity of salt is consumed. And there is no province or city that suffers any lack of it, because of the great amounts extracted in some lands, which are then distributed to others, and none [the passage breaks off here]

entities_mentioned.name: sal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo refers here primarily to salt in its industrial/mineralogical role as a reagent in the patio process (amalgamation) for silver ore refining, rather than in a dietary or strictly medical context. Salt was a critical input in colonial Andean silver metallurgy.

entities_mentioned.name: salinas naturales
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natural salt flats / evaporite salt deposits
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to naturally occurring salt sources in Peru, including coastal salt flats and inland saline deposits. Peru has numerous such sites, including the famous Maras salt pans near Cusco.

entities_mentioned.name: metales de plata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silver ores (likely argentite/acanthite, Ag2S, and others)
entities_mentioned.notes: The silver ores of colonial Peru, most famously from Potosí. Salt was used in the patio amalgamation process introduced by Bartolomé de Medina c. 1554, in which salt reacted with copper sulfate and mercury to extract silver.

results.title_inferred: Building Stones: Quarried and Volcanic Varieties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
De las piedras para fábricas

Toda suerte de piedra franca y berroqueña que es suave de labrar es la que decimos ser a propósito para edificios de cantería, de la cual hay escogidas canteras en todas estas Indias; unas piedras se hallan muy livianas y cavernosas, a modo de un panal o piedra pómez, que no se hunde en el agua, la cual es maravillosa para fábricas, señaladamente para murallas y fortalezas; semejantes a ellas hay otros muchos géneros de piedras.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
On Building Stones

Every sort of freestone [piedra franca] and granite [berroqueña] that is easy to work is what we call suitable for ashlar [cantería] construction, of which there are choice quarries throughout these Indies. Some stones are found to be very light and porous, in the manner of a honeycomb or pumice stone [piedra pómez], which does not sink in water; this is marvellous for construction, particularly for walls and fortifications. Similar to these there are many other kinds of stones.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra franca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Freestone — any fine-grained, easily worked sedimentary or volcanic stone (commonly limestone or sandstone)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Spanish architectural term for soft, workable quarried stone.

entities_mentioned.name: berroqueña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Granite or granitic stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Term used in colonial Spanish for hard, coarse-grained igneous stone; cognate with 'granito berroqueño' still used in modern Spanish geology.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra pómez
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pumice (volcanic glass foam, Pumicite spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes its low density (does not sink in water) and praises it for military construction — walls and fortifications. Pumice is abundant in the Andes due to volcanic activity.

De la piedra bezar

La piedra bezar está compuesta de unas escamas o costras como cáscaras de bellotas, unas sobre otras, de las cuales la primera es muy lisa y lustrosa. Hállanse estas piedras de diferente tamaño, desde tan pequeñas como garbanzos hasta de la grandeza de un huevo de gallina; y algunas (aunque raras) tan grandes como el puño y de una libra y más de peso (20). results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX
On the Bezoar Stone

The bezoar stone is composed of layers or crusts resembling acorn shells, one upon another, of which the outermost is very smooth and lustrous. These stones are found in different sizes, from as small as chickpeas up to the size of a hen's egg; and some (though rare) as large as a fist and weighing one pound or more (20). Their shape is highly varied: some are round; others, oval and flat, and of such diverse [forms...]

entities_mentioned.name: piedra bezar (bezoar stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bezoar — a calcified concretion found in the gastrointestinal tract of ruminant animals (most commonly Capra aegagrus, the wild goat, or New World camelids such as Lama guanicoe); highly prized in early modern medicine as an antidote and alexipharmic
entities_mentioned.notes: The laminated, concentric structure Cobo describes (layers resembling acorn-cup scales) is anatomically accurate for true animal bezoars. The size range he gives — from chickpea to hen's egg, occasionally fist-sized — is also consistent with known specimens. The text is cut off mid-sentence at 'de tan di[versa forma]', indicating the segment continues in a subsequent part.

results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXII
On Pearls

The first two explorers of the seas and coasts of America were also those who first discovered the pearls that are found in both seas: Admiral Don Cristóbal Colón, on the coast of the North Sea [Caribbean], and Captain Vasco Núñez de Balboa, on that of the South Sea [Pacific]. For on the third occasion that the Admiral came to the Indies, in the year 1498, sailing along the mainland coast that he discovered on that voyage, he made landfall on the island of Cubagua, ec [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: perlas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pearls, likely from Pinctada imbricata (Atlantic pearl oyster) in the Caribbean and Pinctada mazatlanica or Pteria sterna in the Pacific
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo refers to pearls found in both the Caribbean ('mar del Norte') and the Pacific ('mar del Sur'). Cubagua, off the coast of present-day Venezuela, was historically one of the most important pearl-fishing sites in the New World.

entities_mentioned.name: Cristóbal Colón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Christopher Columbus
entities_mentioned.notes: Columbus's third voyage in 1498 brought him to the coast of present-day Venezuela and the island of Cubagua, where he encountered pearls held by indigenous peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: Vasco Núñez de Balboa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vasco Núñez de Balboa (1475–1519), Spanish conquistador and first European to see the Pacific Ocean from the New World (1513)
entities_mentioned.notes: Balboa crossed the Isthmus of Darién and reached the Pacific in 1513, where he also encountered pearls, including from the Pearl Islands (Archipiélago de las Perlas, Panama).

entities_mentioned.name: isla de Cubagua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cubagua Island, Nueva Esparta State, Venezuela
entities_mentioned.notes: Site of Nueva Cádiz, the first permanent European settlement on the South American mainland, founded largely to exploit its rich pearl oyster beds.

results.translation:
There is another stone very similar to this one [i.e., the white laminar stone preceding this passage], of a golden color tending toward purple, which likewise splits into sheets [leaves/laminae].

The razors, blades, lancets, and all manner of tools that we make of iron, the Indians—both of Peru and of New Spain—were accustomed to make from a certain stone which the Mexicans call itzli [obsidian], and those of Peru call chillca lisa; this stone is transparent, like glass, and is found in three colors: white, black, and blue. entities_mentioned.name: piedra de color de oro tirante a purpúreo (gold-to-purple laminar stone)

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Obsidian (volcanic glass, SiO₂-rich)

El beneficio de azogue es de mucha más riqueza que el de fundición, porque es más copioso y general y se saca con él todo la plata de los metales, por bajos y pobres que sean. Cuando el metal acude de dos onzas para arriba por quintal, se puede beneficiar con ganancia, y de aquí para abajo, es con muy poca o ninguna, particularmente en los asientos de minas que no tienen indios de cédula y repartimiento, o de mita, como llaman en el Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On the Mercury Amalgamation Process

The mercury amalgamation process [beneficio de azogue] is far more productive than smelting [fundición], because it is more abundant in yield and more universally applicable, and by means of it all the silver is extracted from the ores, however low-grade and poor they may be. When the ore yields two ounces or more per hundredweight [quintal], it can be processed profitably; below this threshold, profit is very little or none at all, particularly in mining settlements that do not have Indians assigned by royal warrant and allotment [cédula y repartimiento], or by the mita, as it is called in Peru.

results.translation:

On Copper

Greater quantities of copper mines are found in this kingdom and in many other parts of the Indies than of silver or any other metals. The Indians formerly extracted it in great amounts, on account of which many mines worked in ancient times are still visible today. Because they lacked iron, they forged from this metal their weapons, tools, and instruments for agriculture and various other trades; however, they made no use of it in any application of medicine. At present [they work]

CAPITULO VI

Del mamón

En la isla Española llaman mamón a otro árbol y fruta parecida a la anona. results.translation:
CHAPTER VI

On the Mamón

On the island of Hispaniola they call mamón another tree and fruit resembling the anona [custard apple]. The mamón is a medium-sized tree, about the size of a peach tree [durazno], and of very pleasing appearance; its leaf resembles that of the alder [aliso], being somewhat larger and more channeled, and of a very light green that greatly beautifies the tree. The fruit bears the same name; when it is small and unripe, it resembles an artichoke [alcachofa] on account of its very uneven surface, as though composed of green scales layered one upon another.

De los zapotes.

results.translation:
CHAPTER XII
On Zapotes.

The zapotes are native to New Spain, where five varieties of them are found; the best are the red ones [colorados]. The tree that bears them is large and spreading [copado], like the mamey, with the young shoots [cogollos] very densely leafed; the leaf is a palm's length [approx. 8 inches] long, smooth, stiff, of a dark green, and wider at the tip than in the middle. The fruit is the size of a quince and even as large as a big citron [cidra], spindle-shaped [ahusada], with a thin and tender skin; the flesh is very red and soft [text breaks off].

CHAPTER VII

Concerning Mayta Cápac, Fourth King of the Incas

The governors raised the prince with more indulgence and freedom than the other sons of kings before him had customarily been raised; on account of which Mayta Cápac grew into a mischievous and bold youth, beyond the fact that by his natural disposition he was brave and spirited. CHAPTER IX

Concerning the sixth king of Peru, called Inca Roca

The sixth king of this land was Inca Roca, of whom the Indians say he began the faction [parcialidad] of Hanan-Cusco. However, inasmuch as the division of the city of Cuzco into the two factions [parcialidades] known as Hanan-Cuzco and Hurin-Cuzco had already been established since the time of Manco Cápac, the origin of Hanan-Cuzco cannot properly be attributed to this Inca. Del orden cómo se repartía el ganado manso, y la renta que en él y en la ropa de sus lanas tenían el Inca y la religión; y cómo los cazaderos y montes eran realengos

La misma división tenía hecha el Inca de todo el ganado manso que dellas tierras, aplicando una parte a la religión, a sí otra y a la comunidad otra: y no sólo dividió y separó cada una destas partes, sino también las dehesas y pastos en que se apacentasen, de modo que anduviesen e[...]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIX

On the manner in which tame livestock was distributed, and the revenue that the Inca and the [state] religion derived from it and from cloth made of its wool; and how the hunting grounds and forests were royal [Crown] property

The Inca had made the same division of all the tame livestock of those lands, allocating one portion to religion [i.e., the state religious establishment], another to himself, and another to the community [the common people]. And he not only divided and separated each of these portions, but also the pastures [dehesas] and grazing lands in which they were to be fed, so that they [the herds] might go about [the text breaks off here]

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca — sovereign ruler of Tawantinsuyu (Inca Empire)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'el Inca' to refer to the ruling Inca sovereign and, by extension, to the imperial institution.

entities_mentioned.name: ganado manso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Domesticated camelids — primarily llamas (Lama glama) and alpacas (Vicugna pacos)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Ganado manso' (tame livestock) in the Andean context invariably refers to domesticated camelids, the principal herd animals of the Inca Empire, valued for wool, transport, and sacrifice.

entities_mentioned.name: ropa de sus lanas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Woven textiles from camelid fiber (cumbi and abasca cloth)
entities_mentioned.notes: Camelid wool textiles were a key commodity and store of wealth in the Inca redistributive economy.

entities_mentioned.name: cazaderos y montes realengos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Royal Inca hunting reserves and forests (Crown lands)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the Castilian legal term 'realengos' (Crown/royal property) to describe Inca-controlled hunting territories, drawing a direct analogy to Spanish royal hunting reserves.

entities_mentioned.name: dehesas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Designated pastureland / grazing commons
entities_mentioned.notes: A Spanish term for enclosed or managed pasture, used here to describe Inca-designated grazing territories. Retained in brackets in translation for period legal flavor.

results.title_inferred: The Royal Roads Built by the Incas Across Their Kingdom
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI

De los caminos que hicieron los Incas por todo su reino, y la contribución de gente que para aderezarlos daban las provincias

Tenían hechos los Incas dos caminos reales que tomaban todo el largo de su reino desde la provincia de Quito hasta el reino de Chile, que son novecientas leguas, el uno por los Llanos y costa de la mar, y el otro la tierra adentro por las provincias de la Sierra, desviado de la costa por unas partes treinta leguas y por otras cinco.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI

Concerning the roads that the Incas built throughout their kingdom, and the contribution of labor that the provinces provided for their upkeep

The Incas had constructed two royal roads [caminos reales] that ran the entire length of their kingdom, from the province of Quito to the kingdom of Chile—a distance of nine hundred leagues [leguas]. One road ran along the lowlands [los Llanos] and the sea coast, and the other ran inland through the provinces of the highlands [la Sierra], set back from the coast by as much as thirty leagues in some places and five in others.

entities_mentioned.name: Quito
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quito, Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Northern terminus of the Inca road system as described by Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: Chile
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chile (region/kingdom, roughly modern central Chile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Southern terminus of the Inca road system; the extent referred to likely corresponds to the Maule or Biobío frontier.

entities_mentioned.name: los Llanos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coastal plain of western South America (modern Peruvian and Chilean coast)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the flat coastal desert lowlands running along the Pacific.

entities_mentioned.name: la Sierra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean highland provinces of the Inca Empire
entities_mentioned.notes: The highland road corresponds to the well-documented Qhapaq Ñan (Inca highland trunk road).

Del demás tributo que en servicio personal pagaban a su rey los indios

A los cuales
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII

On the further tribute that the Indians paid to their king in personal service

Beyond the labor that the towns contributed in lieu of a head-tax [pecho] or tribute — namely their work on the farmlands and the raising of livestock belonging to the Inca and to the religion [i.e., the state religious establishment], and in the other trades and tasks we have already described — exceedingly great was the contribution of men and laborers [peones] they furnished for all the ministries and public works carried out throughout the kingdom, both for the service and benefit of the king and for the commonwealth [república]. To these [laborers]

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca, ruler of Tawantinsuyu (the Inca Empire)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'Inca' here to refer both to the king and, by extension, to the state apparatus including its religious institutions.

entities_mentioned.name: pecho
entities_mentioned.notes: Period Castilian fiscal term for a head-tax or direct tribute paid in goods or money; Cobo contrasts it with the Andean mit'a system of labor tribute.

entities_mentioned.name: peones
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to common laborers or foot-workers drafted for state corvée labor, analogous to the Andean mit'a (rotational labor obligation).

Del cómputo del tiempo; de los quipos o memoriales y modo de contar que tenían los indios peruanos

results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII

On the reckoning of time; on the quipus [quipos] or memoranda, and the method of counting used by the Peruvian Indians

The movement of the heavens being, as it is, a thing so admirable, well-known, and manifest to all peoples, there is none so barbarous and rude that, at least when it raises its e[yes...]

entities_mentioned.name: quipos (quipus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quipu — knotted cord recording devices used by Andean peoples, particularly the Inca
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the spelling 'quipos'; the standard modern scholarly spelling is 'quipu' (from Quechua 'khipu'). These knotted cords served as mnemonic and accounting devices, described here by Cobo as 'memoriales' (memoranda or records). No medical content in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: indios peruanos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous peoples of Peru, principally the Inca and subject Andean populations
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's generic term for the native inhabitants of the Viceroyalty of Peru.

CAPITULO II

De las opiniones y fábulas que acerca de su origen, principios del mundo y diluvio universal estaban recebidas desta gente, con las guacas que desto resultaron

Como el total fundamento de toda religión y culto divino sea el conocimiento de la primera causa, ora sea la verdadera, ora falsa y fingida, de quien los hombres creen haber procedido y depender della en la conservación de su ser, y también la noticia del fin y estado que después desta vida esperan tener; por
results.translation:
CHAPTER II

Concerning the opinions and fables received among these people regarding their origin, the beginnings of the world, and the universal flood, together with the sacred sites [guacas] that resulted therefrom

Since the entire foundation of all religion and divine worship is the knowledge of the first cause—whether true, or false and fictitious—from whom men believe themselves to have proceeded and upon whom they believe themselves to depend for the preservation of their being, and likewise the knowledge of the end and state they expect to have after this life; [text continues]

entities_mentioned.name: guacas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: huacas — Andean sacred sites, objects, or beings (Quechua: wak'a)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'guacas' to refer to the sacred shrines, places, or objects venerated by Andean peoples, often arising from mythological events such as origin stories or flood narratives. The term wak'a in Quechua encompasses a broad category of the sacred and numinous.
